content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob play a game. They have an array AAA of NNN elements.

First, Alice picks any index iii (1≤i≤N)(1 \leq i \leq N)(1≤i≤N).

Then, Bob has to pick an index jjj such that ∣i−j∣=1|i-j| = 1∣i−j∣=1 ,i.e , he must pick an element which is adjacent to that of the Alice.

Let the score of the game be ∣Ai−Aj∣|A_i - A_j|∣Ai​−Aj​∣.

Alice wants to minimize the score while Bob wants to maximize the score . Determine the optimal score if they both play optimally.

Note that ∣X∣|X|∣X∣ denotes the absolute value of XXX. For example, ∣4∣=4|4| = 4∣4∣=4 and ∣−7∣=7|-7|= 7∣−7∣=7.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ representing the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the optimal score if both players play optimally.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (the length of the array). The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the optimal score when both players play optimally.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Alice can pick any index; every adjacent pair has difference 0, so the optimal score is 0.\nTest case 2: Alice picks index 3 (value 3). Bob can choose index 2 (value 2) or 4 (value 4); the larger difference is |3-2|=1, so the score is 1.\nTest case 3: Alice picks index 1 (value -10). Bob must pick index 2 (value 10), giving |‑10‑10|=20, which is the minimal possible maximal difference.
Only two elements exist, so Alice must choose one of them and Bob is forced to pick the other. The absolute difference is |5‑(-5)| = 10.
Values of max adjacent difference for each position: i=1 →3, i=2 →5, i=3 →6, i=4 →6, i=5 →3. Alice chooses i=1 or i=5, giving a score of 3.

solution.approach: For each index i Alice could choose, Bob will pick the adjacent index that gives the larger absolute difference. Therefore for every i we compute value_i = max(|Ai - A_{i-1}| if i>1, |Ai - A_{i+1}| if i<N). Alice will choose the i that minimises this value. The answer is min_i value_i. This can be obtained in a single linear scan: compute the two neighbour differences, keep the larger one for the current i, and track the smallest such maximum across all i.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, because we examine each element a constant number of times.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input array.
